quote: Hey. This is John. I'm returning your call. I had, inquired about the Tesla solar panels.
stewart_read: John opens with 'Tesla solar panels' — a product Ion does not sell. The schema is explicit: do NOT reframe the Tesla relationship unless the customer pushes. John does not push here; he moves on cooperatively. Parker's non-reframe is therefore schema-compliant at the setter stage. The residual risk is that John's expectation gap remains live — he may arrive at the closer appointment expecting Tesla-branded panels. This is a handoff flag, not a Parker coaching gap.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker missed an opportunity to proactively clarify the Ion/Tesla relationship and prevent closer-stage confusion.
coaching_implication: No coaching action needed for Parker on this specific moment — he followed schema guidance correctly. Flag in handoff brief so the closer addresses the Tesla product expectation early in the proposal conversation per schema reframe guidance (context.tesla_relationship_clarification).

quote: it can be as much as $500.
stewart_read: John volunteers $500 summer bills unprompted — the highest-value emotional anchor on the call. Parker responds 'Oh, wow.' and moves to a generic savings promise. The schema's capture-and-flip pattern is directly applicable: the $500 number should be turned against the utility, not acknowledged as a data point. This is the floor's universal coaching gap appearing on a call where the customer handed the lever over willingly.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker's acknowledgment was intentionally brief to keep momentum toward the appointment — a strategic choice to avoid over-explaining.
coaching_implication: Show Parker the schema's objection_inversion triggers. The $500 bill is the exact scenario the flip is designed for. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'Five hundred dollars — that's the bill you're paying Pepco every summer whether you use that energy efficiently or not. That's what we're trying to replace.' No fabricated savings numbers needed; the pain the number represents is the leverage.

quote: Oh, okay. So you're talking about somebody actually coming here?
stewart_read: John's surprise reveals Parker confirmed the appointment time before disclosing the format. The correct sequence is format disclosure first, then time confirmation — so the customer is agreeing to what they're actually agreeing to. John accepts the in-person format without resistance, so no damage is done here, but the surprise moment is an avoidable friction point that could go differently with a less cooperative customer.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have assumed in-person was the default and John would infer it — a reasonable assumption on a residential solar call.
coaching_implication: Drill the button_up sequence: format disclosure before time confirmation. A simple fix: 'Our specialist will drop by in person — does tomorrow afternoon work for you?' One sentence eliminates the surprise entirely.

quote: work with you from there. If you like it, we can help you get it going. If if it just doesn't make sense for you, no harm, no foul there.
stewart_read: Parker uses a 'no harm, no foul' close instead of the schema-required 'is that fair?' handshake. The intent is similar — reduce pressure, get verbal commitment — but the 'is that fair?' handshake is a direct verbal confirmation that the customer is agreeing to the appointment on its stated terms. 'No harm, no foul' is a release valve, not a handshake. The schema flags skipping this as predictive of higher no-show rates.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker's phrasing achieves the same psychological goal — reducing pressure — and John's subsequent cooperative responses suggest the appointment is solid.
coaching_implication: The 'is that fair?' handshake is a one-word swap from what Parker already does. In the 1-on-1, play the clip and ask Parker what he thinks the difference is between 'no harm, no foul' and 'is that fair?' The latter gets a yes; the former gets a shrug. A yes is what predicts the sit.

quote: Yeah. I'll text you even after this just with my own number.
stewart_read: Parker offers his personal number for follow-up contact. The schema flags this as a privacy and scale anti-pattern — setters giving out personal numbers creates exposure across the floor and bypasses any systematic follow-up infrastructure. On this single call it's low-stakes; as a floor-wide habit it's a structural problem.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker is building personal rapport and making John feel like he has a direct line — a trust-building move on a cooperative customer.
coaching_implication: This is a floor-level coaching note, not a rep-level failure. Flag for the manager: until Ion's automated bill-capture and follow-up infrastructure ships (per schema roadmap), setters are defaulting to personal numbers. The manager should establish a floor standard for follow-up contact that doesn't require personal number exposure.

quote: And then is that roof just a an an asphalt single roof there?
stewart_read: Parker asks roof type but does not ask roof age. The schema treats roof_type_age as a single required verify item, and the hard DQ rule on roofs over 15 years makes age the more critical of the two data points. Parker captures type (asphalt shingle) but leaves age uncaptured. On a Mount Rainier single-family home, roof age is a real qualification variable — if the roof is over 15 years old, the appointment may need to be re-routed to the roofing team before the closer visit.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have had roof age data from the lead record and didn't need to ask.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Parker what he does when a customer's roof turns out to be 18 years old at the closer visit. If he doesn't have a clear answer, the verify gap is a real risk. Drill the combined question: 'Is that an asphalt shingle roof — and do you know roughly how old it is?' One sentence captures both required data points.

quote: what kinda got you, you know, looking at solar for the home?
stewart_read: Parker asks the interest question correctly and John gives a two-part answer: utility bills and Instagram ads. The bill pain is the primary driver — a strong qualify signal. Parker acknowledges with 'Yeah. Gotcha.' and moves on. The schema flags this as the emotional anchor of the verify section — top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Parker checks the box in under 10 seconds. The Instagram signal is also worth a beat: it means John has been in a consideration phase, not just a reactive inquiry.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker's brevity here is intentional — John is cooperative and the call is moving well, so dwelling on motivation risks over-explaining.
coaching_implication: Show Parker the schema note on verify.interest_in_solar: 'Depth matters more than the answer.' The question was asked correctly; the follow-through was missing. A single probe — 'What's been the biggest driver, the bill itself or just wanting more control over it?' — would have deepened the anchor without slowing the call.

quote: You want that so you can look at my usage over time. Right?
stewart_read: John asks a clarifying question about why Ion needs the bill — specifically whether it's for usage data rather than monetary amount. This is an educated, engaged question from a customer who has been thinking about solar (consistent with the Instagram research signal). Parker answers correctly and concisely. However, this moment also reveals that Parker never asked the schema-required prior_designs question — 'Have you seen a solar design for this home?' John's level of engagement suggests he may have already received a quote from another company, which would be material context for the closer.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have had prior-quote data from the lead record.
coaching_implication: Flag the prior_designs verify gap. In the 1-on-1, ask Parker: 'Do you know if John has already seen a design from another company?' If Parker doesn't know, the closer walks in blind on competitive context. Drill the prior_designs question as a natural follow-on to the bill conversation: 'Have you had a chance to see what a solar design would look like for your home yet, or would this be the first one?'
